frameworks.name: information-seeking behavior
frameworks.one_line: users querying AI vs search engines to acquire political knowledge

frameworks.name: randomised controlled trial
frameworks.one_line: experimental comparison of conversational AI against self-directed Google search

frameworks.name: misinformation and belief updating
frameworks.one_line: political knowledge framed as belief in true vs false claims

core_question: Does using conversational AI for political information-seeking change voters' political knowledge compared to traditional internet search?
key_assumptions: political knowledge can be operationalised as belief in true vs false claims
chatbot use during elections is a meaningful and measurable behaviour
task-directed conversations are comparable to self-directed search as an information channel
manipulations: information source (conversational AI vs self-directed Google search)
key_findings: 32% of chatbot users and 13% of UK voters used conversational AI for political info before the 2024 election
task-directed AI conversations increased belief in true information and decreased belief in misinformation
knowledge gains from AI were comparable to those from self-directed Google search
results held across issues, models, and prompting strategies
interpretations: conversational AI is already a non-trivial channel for political information-seeking
for task-directed political queries, AI is at least as informative as conventional search
